steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Two forces act on an object: 20 N to the right and 8 N to the left. What is the resultant force?
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Many students add the two forces together without thinking about direction. Because the forces act in opposite directions, you subtract: 20 − 8 = 12 N. The resultant acts to the right because the larger force points that way.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! The forces oppose each other, so you subtract: 20 − 8 = 12 N to the right. The resultant always points in the direction of the larger force.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: Forces misconception, AQA Required Practical guidance on resultant forces

steps.title: Resultant Forces
steps.bodyHTML: Forces have both size and direction. The resultant force is the single force that has the same effect as all the individual forces acting on an object combined. When forces act in the same direction, add them. When forces act in opposite directions, subtract them. If the resultant force is zero, the forces are balanced and the object’s motion does not change. A non-zero resultant means forces are unbalanced.
steps.keyEquation: Resultant = sum of forces in one direction − sum of forces in opposite direction
commonErrors: Adding when forces oppose each other
Thinking balanced forces means no forces act

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change force and mass. Watch how acceleration responds in real time.

aqa.questionText: A box has a driving force of 500 N and friction of 200 N. Calculate the resultant force and the acceleration if the mass is 150 kg.

steps.label: Find the resultant force
steps.content:
Subtract opposing forces:
Driving force = 500 N (forward)
Friction = 200 N (backward)
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct resultant force with direction

steps.label: Find the acceleration
steps.content: Use F = ma, rearranged to a = F / m:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct substitution; 1 mark: correct answer with unit

edexcel.questionText: A rocket has thrust 15 000 N upward and weight 12 000 N downward. Find the resultant force.

steps.label: Identify forces and directions
steps.content:
Thrust = 15 000 N (upward)
Weight = 12 000 N (downward)
Forces are in opposite directions.

steps.label: Subtract opposing forces
steps.content: The upward force is larger, so the resultant acts upward:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct calculation; 1 mark: correct answer with direction

ocr-gateway.questionText: A car has engine force 3000 N, air resistance 1200 N, and friction 800 N. Find the resultant force.

steps.label: Add all opposing forces
steps.content:
Engine force = 3000 N (forward)
Air resistance = 1200 N (backward)
Friction = 800 N (backward)
Total backward force = 1200 + 800 = 2000 N

ocr-21c.questionText: Two people push a trolley: 40 N and 60 N in the same direction. A friction force of 30 N opposes the motion. Find the resultant force.

steps.label: Add forces in the same direction
steps.content:
Both pushes act forward:
Total forward force = 40 + 60 = 100 N
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct addition of forward forces

steps.label: Subtract the friction
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: correct subtraction; 1 mark: correct answer with direction

wjec.questionText: A parachutist weighs 700 N. Air resistance is 700 N. Describe the resultant force and the motion of the parachutist.

steps.label: Find the resultant force
steps.content:
Weight = 700 N (downward)
Air resistance = 700 N (upward)
Forces are equal and opposite:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark: resultant force is zero / balanced forces

steps.label: Describe the motion
steps.content: When forces are balanced (resultant = 0 N), the object continues at constant speed. The parachutist falls at a steady speed: this is called terminal velocity.

wjec.finalAnswer: Resultant = 0 N; parachutist moves at constant speed (terminal velocity)

aqa.questionText: A sledge is pulled with 250 N forward. Friction is 100 N. What is the resultant force?
markSchemeSteps: Identify forces: 250 N forward, 100 N backward
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct method · 1 mark correct answer
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is a trap: adding instead of subtracting gives 350 N.

edexcel.questionText: Forces of 6 N and 4 N act in opposite directions on an object. What is the resultant force?
edexcel.commonMistake: Option A is a trap: adding instead of subtracting for opposing forces.

ocr-gateway.source: OCR Gateway style · Resultant forces practice
ocr-gateway.questionText: Three forces act on an object: 50 N to the right, 30 N to the left, and 10 N to the left. What is the resultant force?
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark combining forces in same direction · 1 mark correct resultant with direction
ocr-gateway.commonMistake: Option A adds all three forces ignoring direction. Option C gives the wrong direction.

ocr-21c.source: OCR 21st Century style · Resultant forces practice
ocr-21c.questionText: A boat has engine force 800 N forward and water resistance 800 N backward. What happens to the boat’s motion?
markSchemeSteps: Balanced forces → no change in motion → constant speed ✓
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark identifying balanced forces · 1 mark correct description of motion
ocr-21c.commonMistake: Option D is wrong. balanced forces do not mean the object stops; it continues at the same speed.

wjec.questionText: A trolley has 200 N forward and 50 N friction. What is the resultant force?
wjec.commonMistake: Option A adds the forces instead of subtracting. Option D ignores friction entirely.

equation.canonical: F_resultant = F₁ + F₂ (same direction) or F₁ − F₂ (opposite)
equation.examTip: Always draw a force diagram first. Label every force with its size and direction.

boardNotes.aqa: AQA tests resultant forces with free body diagrams. You must identify all forces before adding or subtracting.
boardNotes.edexcel: Edexcel often combines resultant force with F = ma in multi-step calculations. Always find the resultant first.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: OCR Gateway frequently asks you to calculate resultant from three or more forces. Group forces by direction first.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: OCR 21st Century links resultant forces to real-world contexts like cars and parachutes. Explain direction clearly.
boardNotes.wjec: WJEC often asks ‘describe’ questions about balanced and unbalanced forces. State both the resultant AND the effect on motion.
